elements.sent-2411-001: Building on this, we developed a simple and fast method that serves as a distribution transfer technique, enabling image enhancement as well as low-level editing, e.g. stroke-based relighting and recoloring.
elements.sent-2411-002: We explore the oscillatory behavior observed in inversion methods applied to large-scale text-to-image diffusion models, with a focus on the "Flux" model.
elements.sent-2411-003: By employing a fixed-point-inspired iterative approach to invert real-world images, we observe that the solution does not achieve convergence, instead oscillating between distinct clusters.
elements.sent-2411-004: Through both toy experiments and real-world diffusion models, we demonstrate that these oscillating clusters exhibit notable semantic coherence.
elements.sent-2411-005: We offer theoretical insights, showing that this behavior arises from oscillatory dynamics in rectified flow models.
elements.sent-2411-006: Building on this understanding, we introduce a simple and fast distribution transfer technique that facilitates image enhancement, stroke-based recoloring, as well as visual prompt-guided image editing.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, we provide quantitative results demonstrating the effectiveness of our method for tasks such as image enhancement, makeup transfer, reconstruction quality, and guided sampling quality.
elements.sent-2411-008: Higher-quality examples of videos and images are available at \href{https://yanyanzheng96.github.io/oscillation_inversion/ {this link .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, the underlying latent structure of rectified flow-based models presents unique challenges, as it differs fundamentally from the layered manifold structure of DDPMs~\cite{ho2020denoising shaped by a parameterized Markov chain.}
elements.sent-2411-010: This distinction makes previous inversion techniques, such as DDIM inversion~\cite{song2020denoising , and editing methods like SDEdit~\cite{meng2021sdedit , less viable.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Therefore, adopting a new perspective for understanding and navigating the latent space of these flow-based models is essential for enabling more effective inversion and image manipulation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-012: These clusters are semantically meaningful and can be leveraged for image optimization and editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-013: This behavior contrasts with the fixed-point methods used in DDIM\cite{aidi, garibi2024renoise , which are primarily designed to mitigate numerical accumulation errors at each step, ensuring smooth convergence to a single, stable solution.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The oscillatory nature observed in rectified flow-based models, however, opens up new possibilities for iterative refinement and enhanced flexibility in image inversion tasks.
elements.sent-2411-015: The inverted latents oscillate among several clusters, which can serve as local latent distributions, facilitating effective semantic-based image optimization.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, we generalize this fixed-point method in three ways for broader downstream applications: 1) Group Inversion: We invert a group of images simultaneously, rather than a single image, enabling semantic guidance and blending across images.
elements.sent-2411-017: These extensions make Oscillation Inversion a versatile tool for various image manipulation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-018: We propose Oscillation Inversion to facilitate one-step inversion to any timestep in rectified flow-based diffusion models for the diverse manipulation of latents.
elements.sent-2411-019: Additionally, we present three extensions that enable diverse user inputs for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-020: We propose a theoretical framework to explain and validate the oscillation phenomenon, which aligns closely with the results obtained from rectified flow trained on a toy dataset.
elements.sent-2411-021: Extensive experiments on various downstream tasks, such as image restoration and enhancement, stroke based make up transfer, validate our theoretical findings and demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on both perceptual quality and data fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-022: Diffusion models~\cite{rombach2022high, saharia2022photorealistic, ramesh2022hierarchical generate data by a stochastic differential equation (SDE)-based denoising process and probability flow ordinary differential equations (ODE)~\cite{song2020score, lipman2022flow, lipman2022flow, salimans2022progressive, song2023consistency improves sampling efficiency by formulating the denoising process into a ODE-based process.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, probability flow ODE-based methods suffer from the computational expense of denoising via numerical integration with small step sizes.
elements.sent-2411-024: To address these issues, some simulation-free flow models have emerged, e.g. flow matching~\cite{lipman2022flow and rectified flow~\cite{liu2022flow .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Flow matching introduces a training objective for continuous normalizing flows~\cite{chen2018neural to regress the vector field of a probability path.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Rectified flow learns a transport map between two distributions through constraining the ODE to follow the straight transport paths.
elements.sent-2411-027: Since the latent structure of flow models differs fundamentally from the layered manifold structure of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs)~\cite{ho2020denoising , it is valuable to explore the intrinsic characteristics of the flow models' latent space.. Diffusion-based Inversion.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The rise of diffusion models~\cite{rombach2022high, saharia2022photorealistic, ramesh2022hierarchical has unlocked significant potential of inversion methods for real image editing, which are primarily categorized into DDPM~\cite{ho2020denoising based~\cite{wu2023latent, hubermanspiegelglas2024editfriendlyddpmnoise and DDIM~\cite{song2020denoising based methods~\cite{pan2023effective, garibi2024renoise, li2024source, meiri2023fixed .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: While DDPM-based methods yield impressive editing results, they are hindered by their inherently time-consuming and stochastic nature, due to the random noise introduced across a large number of inversion steps~\cite{wu2023latent, hubermanspiegelglas2024editfriendlyddpmnoise .}
elements.sent-2411-030: DDIM-based methods utilize the DDIM sampling strategy to enable a more deterministic inversion process, substantially reducing computational overhead and time.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, the linear approximation behind DDIM often leads to error propagation, resulting in reconstruction inaccuracy and content loss, especially when classifier-free guidance (CFG) is applied~\cite{mokady2023null .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Recent approaches,~\cite{wallace2023edict, mokady2023null, pan2023effective, miyake2023negative, han2023improving, Hong_2024_CVPR , address these issues by aligning the diffusion and reverse diffusion trajectories through the optimization of null-text tokens~\cite{mokady2023null or prompt embeddings~\cite{han2023improving, miyake2023negative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: EDICT~\cite{wallace2023edict and BDIA~\cite{zhang2023exact introduce invertible neural network layers to enhance computational efficiency and inversion accuracy, though these methods suffer from notably longer inversion times.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: To tackle this, recent works~\cite{meiri2023fixed, pan2023effective ~\cite{garibi2024renoise, li2024source have adopted fixed-point iteration for each inversion step, mitigating numerical error accumulation and ensuring smooth convergence to a single, stable solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Interestingly, when applied to rectified flow-based methods, the sequence of fixed-point iterates oscillates between several semantically meaningful clusters, presenting significant potential for downstream applications.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/pipelinev1.pdf} \caption{In the left figure (a), fixed-point iteration causes oscillation, leading to subdomains with opposite features in the case of the brown-skinned girl, resulting in more tan and red tones. In the right figure (b), we demonstrate how this oscillation can be customized to achieve desired separations, such as changes in hair color. } \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-036: Rectified flow~\cite{liu2022flow is a novel generative approach that facilitates smooth transitions between two distributions, denoted $\pi_0$ for noise and $\pi_1$ for target, by solving ordinary differential equations (ODEs).}
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, for $X_0 \sim \pi_0$ and $X_1 \sim \pi_1$, the transition between $x_0$ and $x_1$ is defined through an interpolation given by $X_t = (1-t)X_0 + tX_1$ for $t \in [0, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this section, we first formulate the inversion problem for rectified flow-based models (Sec.\ref{sec:problem ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: To address this, we introduce Oscillation Inversion, which constructs one-step inversion using fixed-point iteration (Sec.\ref{sec:oscillation ).}
elements.sent-2411-040: We then propose fine-tuned inversion to enable controllable latent structures (Sec.\ref{sec:finetune ), enabing make-up transfer task in Sec.\ref{section:makeup , followed by post-inversion optimization that leverages the differentiation structure introduced by one-step inversion (Sec.\ref{sec:postoptim ), enabling visual prompt-guided sampling(Sec.\ref{sec:optim_app ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The concept behind our general method is depicted in Figure \ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-042: In practice, the large flow model in the context of generative modeling operates within the latent space of a Variational Autoencoder (VAE)~\cite{kingma2013auto , utilizing an encoder $E: \mathbb{R ^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^n$ and a decoder $D: \mathbb{R ^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^d$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The sampling process begins from Gaussian noise \( z_T \sim \mathcal{N (0, \mathbf{I ) \), and the latent variable is progressively refined through a sequence of transformations.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The forward generative process is defined by the following iterative formula starting from $t=T$ all the way back to $t=0$: \begin{equation}z_{t-1} = z_t + \left( \sigma_{t-1} - \sigma_t \right) v_{\theta}(z_t, \sigma_t),\end{equation} where $v_{\theta (z_t, \sigma_t)$ represents the learned velocity field parameterized by a transformer with weights $\theta$, and $\sigma_t$ is a monotonically increasing time step scaling function depending on time $t$ with $\sigma_0=0$ and $\sigma_T=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Here, $T$ denotes the total number of discredited timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-046: The final latent variable, $z_0$, is the output ready for decoding.
elements.sent-2411-047: The inversion problem involves seeking for the initial noise $z_T$ given an observed pixel image $I$ with corresponding latent encoding $y \in \mathbb{R ^d$, such that generating from $z_T$ using the flow model described above allows us to either reconstruct $y$ or apply desired modifications to it.}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, the gradual process of sampling from Gaussian noise to the original $y$ diminishes the advantage of GAN-like one-step mappings for direct latent space optimization.
elements.sent-2411-049: To address this, unlike tackling with the initial noise at $t=T$, we introduce the assumption that, at a selected intermediate timestep $t_0$, there exists a direct one-step mapping from the noisy latent at timestep $t_0$ to the clean latent at timestep $0$ via a ``jumping'' transformation.
elements.sent-2411-050: More specifically, assuming $y$ is the latent code to recover, we aim to figure out the intermediate latent code $z_{t_0}$ satisfying \begin{equation}z_{t_0} + \left( \sigma_0 - \sigma_{t_0} \right) v_{\theta}(z_{t_0}, \sigma_{t_0})= y. \label{eq: jump}\end{equation} Solving Eq.~(\ref{eq: jump ) is non-trivial, and in the following sections, we will describe how we find a set of approximated solutions using iterative method and analyze its oscillating properties.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{fig/theory_refine.pdf} \caption{By training flow matching on the distribution displayed in Fig. \ref{fig:mixturegaussian}, we demonstrate that the oscillation inversion phenomenon observed in large flow models aligns well with that seen in toy data. The period $m$ indicates that our group inversion starts with $m$ initial $y$s. Row (a) shows the trajectory of inverted latents, $z_{t_0}^{(k+1)}$. Row (b) shows the one-step prediction back to the input space from these latents. Row (c) presents the quantitative distance along the latents' trajectory, reflecting clear periodic patterns.} \label{fig:theory_refine}
elements.sent-2411-051: To address the inversion problem, we employ a fixed-point iteration method to approach the solution of Eq.~(\ref{eq: jump ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Instead of directly seeking a point $z_{t_0}$ such that applying the one-step generative process as described in the left side of (\ref{eq: jump ) yields the target latent $y$, we define an iterative process that refines our approximation of the inverted latent code.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We define the fixed-point iteration as: \begin{equation}z^{(k+1)}_{t_0} = y - (\sigma_0 - \sigma_{t_0}) v_{\theta}(z^{(k)}_{t_0}, \sigma_{t_0}), \label{eq: iterative formula}\end{equation} with the initial condition $z^{(0) _{t_0 = y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The sequence $\{ z^{(k) _{t_0 \}_{k=0 ^{\infty}$ represents successive approximations of the inverted latent code at timestep $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: As shwon in Figure~\ref{fig:theory_refine , rather than converging to a single point as suggested by Banach's Fixed-Point Theorem~\cite{banach1922operations , we empirically observed that the sequence $\{ z^{(k) _{t_0 \}_{k=0 ^{\infty}$ generally oscillates among several clusters in the latent space.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Each cluster corresponds to a semantically concentrated region that shares similar low-level features.
elements.sent-2411-057: This oscillatory behavior can be harnessed to explore different variations of the input image, providing a richer inversion that captures multiple aspects of the data.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{fig/trigger.pdf} \caption{This is an example of group inversion, where the high-quality distribution is triggered by two degenerate distributions. We also apply this method to large-scale experiments in Section \ref{sec:largescale}.} \label{fig:trigger}
elements.sent-2411-058: Building upon the fixed-point method, we introduce group inversion that can trigger more stable oscillation phenomena by inverting a set of images simultaneously in a periodic fashion as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:trigger .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Suppose we obtain their corresponding latent encodings $y_1,\dots, y_m$ from a collection of images $I_1,\dots, I_m$ using the VAE encoding.
elements.sent-2411-060: We could perform the iteration on the group: \begin{equation}z_{t_0}^{(k+1)} = y_{(k\bmod m)} - (\sigma_0 - \sigma_{t_0}) v_{\theta}(z_{t_0}^{(k)}, \sigma_{t_0}), \label{eq:group inversion}\end{equation} with initial conditions $z_{t_0 ^{(0) = y_{(1 \bmod m)}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: By inverting the images together, we enable interactions between their latent representations during the iteration process.
elements.sent-2411-062: Our experimental findings indicate that this collective inversion induces more diverse oscillatory clusters in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-063: Also, the oscillation phenomenon observed in the flow model trained on a toy distribution transitions from a large central Gaussian to a mixture of four smaller Gaussians(Fig. \ref{fig:mixturegaussian ), as showed in Figure \ref{fig:theory_refine This behavior aligns closely with the results from our experiments on larger models.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:trigger , we find that group inversion can be used as a domain transfer technique to obtain higher-quality image clusters by mixing two lower-quality clusters.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Based on this, we also validate this technique on a larger-scale experiment in Section \ref{sec:largescale .}
elements.sent-2411-066: While the fixed-point iteration method introduced earlier reveals oscillations that segment the latent space into separate clusters, these clusters are not directly controllable.
elements.sent-2411-067: To overcome this limitation, we propose finetuned inversion, a simple fine-tuning step that adjusts the inversion direction, allowing the separated clusters to align with customized semantics.
elements.sent-2411-068: This approach can also induce more diverse oscillatory separations in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-069: Given an input image $I$ with its encoded latent representation $y$, we consider the image $\tilde{I}$ modified from $I$ that reflects desired editing, such as alterations made using off-the-shelf masking and inpainting models or customized doodling edits.
elements.sent-2411-070: For example, if original $I$ is an image of a girl with black hair, $\tilde{I}$ could be a roughly edited version where the girl's hair is doodled to appear purple.
elements.sent-2411-071: Importantly, the edited image $\tilde{I}$ does not have to be photo-realistic nor perfect.
elements.sent-2411-072: Encoding the edited image $\tilde{I}$ to obtain $\tilde{y}$, our goal is to fine-tune the parameters $\theta$ of the velocity field network $v_{\theta}$ so that the inversion process aligns with the desired modifications.
elements.sent-2411-073: The fine-tuning optimization problem could be formulated as $ \underset{\theta {minimize \mathcal{L _{\text{finetune = \left\| v_{\theta (y, \sigma_t) - v^{gt (\tilde{y , \sigma_t) \right\|_2^2 $, where $v_{\theta}$ is the velocity field parameterized by $\theta$ that we aim to finetune, and $v^{gt}$ is the pretrained velocity field with frozen weights.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: An example of visual results can be seen in Figure \ref{fig:gradual .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{fig/finetune_gradual_.pdf} \caption{Gradual evolution of two oscillating distributions occurs under the influence of visual prompts during fine-tuning. We utilize this method in the experiments discussed in Section \ref{section:makeup}. } \label{fig:gradual}
elements.sent-2411-075: Through force aligning $v_{\theta (y)$ and $v^{gt (\tilde{y )$ via optimizing over $\theta$, we effectively put strong guidance on the inversion direction so that the resulting oscillatory clusters obtained as described in Sec.~\ref{sec:oscillation would be pulled towards the semantics specified by the reference image $\tilde{I}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Following the fixed-point iterations and optional fine-tuning, we perform a post-inversion optimization to further refine the identified latent code $z_{t_0}$.
elements.sent-2411-077: In Theorem \ref{theorem_mean , we theoretically demonstrate that the mean of the formulated clusters serves as an approximation for a one-step exact inversion from a single input image.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Practically, given an input image, we find that optimization can be performed using either the averaged center among clusters or the individual cluster centers.
elements.sent-2411-079: The former directly facilitates visual guidance, while the latter incorporates cluster regularization.
elements.sent-2411-080: Due to space constraints, we focus on the averaged approach in this section and refer readers to the appendix for details on the cluster-based method.
elements.sent-2411-081: The optimization problem is formulated as: \begin{equation}\underset{z_{t_0}}{min} \ \mathcal{L}_{rgb}\circ D \big(z_{t_0} + (\sigma_0 - \sigma_{t_0}) v_{\theta}(z_{t_0}, \sigma_{t_0})\big),\end{equation} where $\circ$ represents function composition operator while $D(\cdot)$ is the VAE decoder that converts the latent code back to the pixel space, $\mathcal{L _{\text{rgb }$ is a customized loss function defined directly on the decode pixel image space designed based on the specific application or desired attributes.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: In Sec \ref{sec:optim_app , we show that this post-one-step inversion optimization can be used for image interpolation and even works on mesh surfaces with underlying geometric constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In this section, consider a simplified mixed-Gaussian setup trained with an ideal model within rectified flow framework to explain the oscillatory behavior observed in the empirical experiments of fixed-point inversion.
elements.sent-2411-084: We aim to show that, under certain reasonable assumptions, 1.
elements.sent-2411-085: To align with the notation of the ``Flux'' model's time schedule and the notation introduced in Section~\ref{sec:problem , we use a flipped notation here: we denote the source domain as $\pi_1$ and the target domain, which the generation is heading towards, as $\pi_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The pure noise distribution $\pi_1$ is assumed to be the $d$-dimensional standard normal distribution, and the target distribution $\pi_0$ is a Gaussian mixture distribution given by components $\mathcal{N (\mu_c,\Sigma_c)$ and corresponding coefficient $\phi_c$ for $c=1,\dots, c_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Equivalently, the PDF of $\pi_0$ is determined by \begin{equation}P_{\pi_0} = \sum_{c=1}^{c_0} \phi_c\cdot P_{N(\mu_c,\Sigma_c)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-088: Moreover, we consider the ideal case where $v^X$ is the precise solution to Eq.~\ref{eq: optimization formula of rectified flow , which can be written as \begin{equation}v^{X}(x,t)=\mathbb{E}[X_0-X_1\mid (1-t)X_1+tX_0=x]\end{equation} as discussed in \cite{liu2022flow .}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Here $X_t\sim\pi_t$ for $0\leq t\leq 1$, while the derived random variable $X_t=tX_1+(1-t)X_0$ is subject to another Gaussian mixture distribution.
elements.sent-2411-090: Let $\gamma=t_0$ be the intermediate timestamp that we are interested in.
elements.sent-2411-091: The inversion problem Eq.~\ref{eq: jump is equivalent to figuring out the fixed points $z$ of function $f(z;y,\gamma)$, which is defined by \begin{equation}f(z;y,\gamma) := y - (\sigma_0 - \sigma_{\gamma}) v_{\theta}(z, \sigma_{\gamma}) \nonumber = y + \gamma v_{\theta}(z, \sigma_{\gamma}) , \label{eq: formula of f}\end{equation} in which we assume $\sigma(t)$ is set to trivial $t$ without losing generality.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}f(z;y,\gamma) := y - (\sigma_0 - \sigma_{\gamma}) v_{\theta}(z, \sigma_{\gamma}) \nonumber = y + \gamma v_{\theta}(z, \sigma_{\gamma}) , \label{eq: formula of f}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-092: In the rest of this section we may write $f(z)$ with ignoring $y$ and $\gamma$ whenever the context is clear.
elements.sent-2411-093: By fixed point method, we could seek fixed points of Eq.~\ref{eq: formula of f with iterative formula \begin{equation}z^{(k+1)}:=f(z^{(k)}) \label{eq: def of zk}\end{equation} with some initial $z^{(0)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section, we present a series of theorems that collectively demonstrate the instability of the function \( f(z) \) near its roots under certain mild conditions.
elements.sent-2411-095: Specifically, we examine the magnitude of the spectral norm of the Jacobian \( \|J_f(z)\| \) at the root \( z \).
elements.sent-2411-096: First, we establish a foundational result that relates \( f(\cdot) \) to the conditional expectation involving the source and target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-097: Let \( \pi_1 \) be the source distribution and \( \pi_0 \) be the target distribution that the rectified flow transports, following the notations from Section~\ref{theory .}
elements.sent-2411-098: As in Eq.~\ref{eq: formula of f , the function $f$ is given by $ f(z) = y +\gamma v^X(z,\gamma)$ where $ v^X(x,t) = \mathbb{E [X_0 - X_1 \mid X_t = x]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Then we provide the explicit form of $v^X(x,t)$: { \begin{equation}v^X(x,t) = \frac{1}{t^d \cdot \pi_t(x)} \int_{z} \pi_0(z) \cdot \pi_1\left( \frac{x - (1 - t) z}{t} \right) \cdot z \, dz, \label{Eq:conditional expectation of x1}\end{equation} where $\pi_t(x)$ is the probability density function of $X_t$.}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}v^X(x,t) = \frac{1}{t^d \cdot \pi_t(x)} \int_{z} \pi_0(z) \cdot \pi_1\left( \frac{x - (1 - t) z}{t} \right) \cdot z \, dz, \label{Eq:conditional expectation of x1}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-100: Next, we explore the local convergence behavior of \( f(z) \) when the target distribution is a mixture of Gaussians and the source distribution is a standard Gaussian as displayed in Fig \ref{fig:mixturegaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-101: This setting allows us to analyze the properties of the Jacobian of \( f(z) \).
elements.sent-2411-102: Let $z_0$ be one of the fixed points for $f(z)$, we show that under mild conditions the Jacobian \( J_f(z_0) = \nabla_z v^X(z_0,t) \) has at least one singular value greater than 1, or equivalently, $\|J_f(z_0)\|>1$ where $\|\cdot\|$ represents the matrix operator norm, implying that the iterative process will never converge to $z_0$ in a stable manner.
elements.sent-2411-103: The model assumption in Theorem~\ref{theorem: divergence is reasonable when modeling the process from a pure random noise distribution to one of several potential clusters with each cluster corresponding to a distinct image class.}
elements.sent-2411-104: This result implies that \( f(z) \) exhibits instability near its roots due to the large singular values of its Jacobian.
elements.sent-2411-105: The instability leads to multiple roots forming compact clusters, each associated with an attraction field.
elements.sent-2411-106: Points within this field are drawn toward the corresponding cluster but never converge to its center.
elements.sent-2411-107: To illustrate the impact of this instability on iterative methods, we present the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-108: Suppose that the equation $f(z)=z$ has a unique solution \( z^* \in \mathbb{R ^n \).}
elements.sent-2411-109: Consider the fixed-point iteration defined by \begin{equation}z_{i+1} = f(z_i),\end{equation} with an initial point \( z_0 \) close to \( z^* \).
elements.sent-2411-110: Then under mild conditions, the sequence \( \{ z_i\} \) as defined by Eq.~(\ref{eq: def of zk ) oscillates between two compact clusters with means \( z' \) and \( z'' \), which are significantly different.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Furthermore, the average of these cluster means approximates the solution \( z^* \): \begin{equation}\frac{z' + z''}{2} \approx z^*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: This theorem demonstrates that due to the instability near the root, the iterative process does not converge directly to \( z^* \) but instead oscillates between clusters.
elements.sent-2411-113: The average of these clusters approximates the exact solution of the inversion problem in Equation~\ref{eq: jump .}
elements.sent-2411-114: In summary, these theorems collectively establish that under mild conditions, \( f(z) \) not only exhibits instability near its fixed points but also possesses a set of clusters derived from the unstable fixed point.
elements.sent-2411-115: Each cluster is compact, with its mean approximating the underlying solution, and is associated with an attraction field that draws points toward it without stable convergence to its center.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{fig/theory_mean.pdf} \caption{Results on our toy experiemnt shows by taking the average among clusters, we achieve near-exact one-step inversion, as demonstrated in Theorem \ref{theorem_mean}.} \label{fig:period}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/makeupbaseline.png} \caption{stroke makeup example and our enhanced result based on SOTA stylish make up transfer method CSDMT(~\cite{csdmt})} \label{fig:makeup}
elements.sent-2411-116: In this section, we refer to our proposed method, Oscillation Inversion, as OInv.
elements.sent-2411-117: All of our experiments are based on the `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell' checkpoint.
elements.sent-2411-118: We run the experiments on a single A6000 GPU with 48GB of memory.
elements.sent-2411-119: All images are cropped and resized to $512 \times 512$ pixels.
elements.sent-2411-120: The oscillation inversion consistently runs for 30 iterations, taking 8.74 seconds per image.
elements.sent-2411-121: For OInv-Finetune, we fine-tune only the Attention modules of the model while freezing all other components, using a consistent 15-step process that takes 10.88 seconds in total.
elements.sent-2411-122: Once a suitable latent representation is found, the reverse steps take 3-5 iterations depending on the settings, and this step is optional.
elements.sent-2411-123: Image restoration and enhancement can be seen as a specialized editing task that aims to recover an underlying clean image with high fidelity and detail from degraded measurements.
elements.sent-2411-124: Existing inversion methods, such as BlindDPS~\cite{chihaoui2024blind , often achieve good perceptual quality but tend to compromise on fidelity to the original image.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Meanwhile, current image restoration techniques and enhancers, like ILVR~\cite{choi2021ilvr , struggle with real-world blind scenarios where the type of degradation is unknown or undefined.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Image enhancement is a challenging problem, as it involves transferring a degenerated distribution to a natural image distribution.
elements.sent-2411-127: To demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in discovering high-quality distributions, as discussed in Section \ref{sec:oscillation , we perform both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.}
elements.sent-2411-128: We use real-world degraded (low-quality) images for the qualitative assessment and apply simulated noise, blur, and low-resolution degradation to the CelebA validation dataset~\cite{liu2015deep for the quantitative analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-129: For the latter, we follow the protocols of previous studies, using metrics like PSNR and LPIPS to measure performance.
elements.sent-2411-130: We compare our approach against existing image restoration and enhancement methods, including BlindDPS, DIP, GDP, and BIRD~\cite{chung2023parallel, ulyanov2018deep, GDP_blur, chihaoui2024blind .}
elements.sent-2411-131: Positioned as a post-processing image enhancement technique, our method is compared to Piscart, chosen for its strong identity preservation and efficient batch processing.

Then, \begin{equation}E[X_1 \mid X_t = x] = \frac{N(x)}{D(x)}.\end{equation}.

elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}J(x) = \frac{\left( \dfrac{1}{t} \int \pi_1(z) \cdot \nabla_u \pi_0(u) \cdot z \, dz \right) D(x) - N(x) \left( t^{d-1} \int \pi_1(z) \cdot \nabla_u \pi_0(u) \, dz \right)}{[D(x)]^2}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-174: Note that \( \nabla_u \pi_0(u) = -\pi_0(u) \Sigma_0^{-1 (u - \mu) \), where \( \Sigma_0 \) is the covariance matrix of \( \pi_0 \), and \( \mu \) is the mean (which is approximately zero due to the assumption that \( \pi_0 \) is centered around the origin).}
elements.sent-2411-176: For simplicity, let \( \pi_0 \) be a single Gaussian with mean zero and covariance \( \Sigma_0 = \sigma_0^2 I \).. Then, \begin{equation}\Sigma_0^{-1} = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} I.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-177: Substitute back: \begin{equation}\nabla_x N(x) = -\frac{1}{t \sigma_0^2} \int \pi_1(z) \cdot \pi_0(u) \cdot u \cdot z \, dz,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\nabla_x D(x) = -\frac{t^{d-1}}{\sigma_0^2} \int \pi_1(z) \cdot \pi_0(u) \cdot u \, dz.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-178: Since both \( \pi_1(z) \) and \( \pi_0(u) \) are Gaussian densities, we can compute the integrals explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-179: Recall that \begin{equation}N(x) = \int_{R^d} \pi_1(z) \cdot \pi_0\left( \frac{x - (1 - t) z}{t} \right) \cdot z \, dz,\end{equation} \begin{equation}D(x) = t^d \cdot \pi_t(x) = t^d \cdot \int_{R^d} \pi_1(z) \cdot \pi_0\left( \frac{x - (1 - t) z}{t} \right) \, dz.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-180: Let \( u = \dfrac{x - (1 - t) z {t \), so \( z = \dfrac{x - t u {1 - t \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Since \( \pi_1(z) \) and \( \pi_0(u) \) are standard Gaussian densities, we have \begin{equation}\pi_1(z) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} \| z \|^2 \right),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\pi_0(u) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} \| u \|^2 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-182: The product \( \pi_1(z) \cdot \pi_0(u) \) becomes \begin{equation}\pi_1(z) \cdot \pi_0(u) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^d} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} \left( \| z \|^2 + \left\| \frac{x - (1 - t) z}{t} \right\|^2 \right) \right).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-189: Let \( f: \mathbb{R ^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^n \) be a continuously differentiable function, and let \( y \in \mathbb{R ^n \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Suppose that the equation \begin{equation}y = z - f(z)\end{equation} has a unique solution \( z^* \in \mathbb{R ^n \).. Assume the following:.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The Jacobian matrix \( J_f(z^*) \) exists and has eigenvalues such that there are components where \( \lambda_j = -1 \).
elements.sent-2411-192: The higher-order terms in the Taylor expansion of \( f(z) \) around \( z^* \) are non-negligible but small enough to keep the iterates bounded.
elements.sent-2411-193: Consider the fixed-point iteration defined by \begin{equation}z_{i+1} = y + f(z_i),\end{equation} with an initial point \( z_0 \) close to \( z^* \).
elements.sent-2411-194: Then, the sequence \( \{ z_i \} \) oscillates between two compact clusters with means \( \bar{z _a \) and \( \bar{z _b \), which are significantly different.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Furthermore, the average of these cluster means approximates the solution \( z^* \): \begin{equation}\frac{\bar{z}_a + \bar{z}_b}{2} \approx z^*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-196: Define the error vector at iteration \( i \) as \begin{equation}e_i = z_i - z^*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-197: Since \( z^* \) satisfies \( z^* = y + f(z^*) \), the iteration becomes \begin{equation}z_{i+1} = z^* + f(z^* + e_i) - f(z^*).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-198: Using the Taylor expansion of \( f(z^* + e_i) \) around \( z^* \), we have \begin{equation}f(z^* + e_i) = f(z^*) + J_f(z^*) e_i + R(e_i),\end{equation} where \( J_f(z^*) \) is the Jacobian of \( f \) at \( z^* \), and \( R(e_i) \) represents the higher-order remainder terms.
elements.sent-2411-199: Substituting back into the iteration: \begin{equation}z_{i+1} = z^* + J_f(z^*) e_i + R(e_i).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-200: Thus, the error at iteration \( i+1 \) is \begin{equation}e_{i+1} = z_{i+1} - z^* = J_f(z^*) e_i + R(e_i).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-201: Let’s perform an eigenvalue decomposition of \( J_f(z^*) \): \begin{equation}J_f(z^*) = Q \Lambda Q^{-1},\end{equation} where \( \Lambda \) is a diagonal matrix of eigenvalues \( \lambda_j \), and \( Q \) is the matrix of corresponding eigenvectors.
elements.sent-2411-202: Transform the error vector into the eigenvector basis: \begin{equation}\tilde{e}_i = Q^{-1} e_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-203: Then the error recurrence relation becomes \begin{equation}\tilde{e}_{i+1} = \Lambda \tilde{e}_i + Q^{-1} R(Q \tilde{e}_i).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-205: For components where \( \lambda_j = -1 \), the linear part of the recurrence is \begin{equation}\tilde{e}_{i+1,j} = -\tilde{e}_{i,j} + r_{i,j},\end{equation} where \( r_{i,j \) is the \( j \)-th component of \( Q^{-1 R(Q \tilde{e _i) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Ignoring higher-order terms for a moment, we observe that \begin{equation}\tilde{e}_{i+1,j} \approx -\tilde{e}_{i,j}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-207: This implies that these components of the error vector alternate in sign at each iteration, causing oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-208: Now, consider the higher-order terms \( r_{i,j \).}
elements.sent-2411-209: Although they are small, they prevent the error components from returning exactly to their previous values, introducing a drift over iterations.. Define two clusters:.
elements.sent-2411-210: Cluster A: Iterations where \( i \) is even.
elements.sent-2411-211: The corresponding errors are approximately \begin{equation}\tilde{e}_{2k,j} \approx \tilde{e}_{0,j} + \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} \delta_{2m,j},\end{equation} where \( \delta_{2m,j \) accumulates the effects of \( r_{2m,j \).}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Cluster B: Iterations where \( i \) is odd.
elements.sent-2411-214: The means of the clusters in the transformed space are \begin{equation}\bar{\tilde{e}}_a = \frac{1}{N_a} \sum_{i \in A} \tilde{e}_i \approx \tilde{e}_{0} + \bar{\delta}_a,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\bar{\tilde{e}}_b = \frac{1}{N_b} \sum_{i \in B} \tilde{e}_i \approx -\tilde{e}_{0} + \bar{\delta}_b,\end{equation} where \( \bar{\delta _a \) and \( \bar{\delta _b \) are the average accumulated deviations in each cluster.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Transforming back to the original space: \begin{equation}\bar{e}_a = Q \bar{\tilde{e}}_a, \quad \bar{e}_b = Q \bar{\tilde{e}}_b.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-216: Thus, the means of the clusters in the original space are \begin{equation}\bar{z}_a = z^* + \bar{e}_a, \quad \bar{z}_b = z^* + \bar{e}_b.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-217: Compute the sum of the cluster means: \begin{equation}\bar{z}_a + \bar{z}_b = 2z^* + (\bar{e}_a + \bar{e}_b).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-219: Assuming that \( \bar{\delta _a \) and \( \bar{\delta _b \) are small (since \( R(e_i) \) is small), we have \begin{equation}\bar{e}_a + \bar{e}_b \approx 0.\end{equation}. Therefore, \begin{equation}\bar{z}_a + \bar{z}_b \approx 2z^*.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: The difference between the cluster means is \begin{equation}\bar{z}_a - \bar{z}_b = (\bar{e}_a - \bar{e}_b) = 2 \tilde{e}_{0} + (\bar{\delta}_a - \bar{\delta}_b).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-222: Since \( \tilde{e _{0 \) is not necessarily small, this shows that \( \bar{z _a \) and \( \bar{z _b \) are significantly different.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: Thus, under the given assumptions, the sequence \( \{ z_i \} \) oscillates between two clusters with significantly different means, and the average of these means approximates \( z^* \).
elements.sent-2411-224: After identifying the sub-distribution characterized by the points in the clusters obtained from the oscillations, we use these points as reference samples in a Gaussian Process (GP) framework to serve as regularization in our optimization loss.
elements.sent-2411-225: This approach ensures that the optimized latent code retains the same style and features as the sub-distribution while allowing gradual evolution guided by a customized loss function.
elements.sent-2411-226: The optimization problem is formulated as: \begin{equation}\underset{z_{t_0}}{minimize} \ \mathcal{L}_{rgb}\circ D \big(z_{t_0} + (\sigma_0 - \sigma_{t_0}) v_{\theta}(z_{t_0}, \sigma_{t_0})\big) + \beta \, \mathcal{L}_{GP}(z_{t_0}),\end{equation} where $\circ$ represents function composition operator while $D(\cdot)$ is the VAE decoder that converts the latent code back to the pixel space, $\mathcal{L _{\text{rgb }$ is a customized loss function defined directly on the decode pixel image space designed based on the specific application or desired attributes.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Another loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{GP (\cdot)$ is the Gaussian Process regularization term that encourages $z_{t_0}$ to stay close in distribution to the sub-distribution formed by the oscillatory cluster.}}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{fig/optimize.png} \caption{Illustration of how our Gaussian Regularization Domain Preserving method works. The red skin and purple hair domains are optimized toward minimizing photo loss, resulting in an image that retains these domains while appearing younger.} \label{fig:optimize}
elements.sent-2411-228: The Gaussian Process regularization term $\mathcal{L _{\text{GP (z)$ is computed using a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel to measure similarity between $z_{t_0}$ and the reference points from the sub-distribution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Specifically, the GP loss is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{GP}}(z) := k(z, z) - \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i} k(z, z_i') + \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i,j} k(z_i', z_j') ,\end{equation} where $k(a, b) := \exp\big( -\frac{\| a - b \|^2 {2 l^2 \big)$ is the RBF kernel with scale $l$, $\{ z_i' \}_{i=1 ^N$ are $N$ reference latent codes from the oscillatory cluster (sub-distribution).}}}
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}L_{GP}(z) := k(z, z) - \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i} k(z, z_i') + \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i,j} k(z_i', z_j') ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-230: This GP loss is derived from the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) measure, which quantifies the difference between the distribution of $z_t$ and the sub-distribution represented by $\{ z_i' \}$.
elements.sent-2411-231: By minimizing $\mathcal{L _{\text{GP (z_{t_0 )$, we encourage $z_{t_0}$ to share similar statistical properties with the cluster, thus maintaining stylistic and feature consistency.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: In the overall optimization, the first term $\mathcal{L _{\text{rgb }$ guides the latent code towards satisfying specific goals or attributes defined by the application, such as emphasizing certain visual features or styles in the decoded image.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: The GP regularization term ensures that these changes remain coherent with the characteristics of the sub-distribution, preventing the optimization from deviating too far from the latent space regions that correspond to realistic and semantically meaningful images.
elements.sent-2411-234: By integrating these two components, we achieve a balance between customizing the output according to desired specifications and preserving the inherent style and features of the sub-distribution identified through oscillation inversion.
elements.sent-2411-235: This method allows for controlled manipulation of the generated images while maintaining high fidelity and coherence with the original data manifold.
elements.sent-2411-236: We present the qualitative visual results of guided sampling using rough visual prompts in the first three columns of Fig.\ref{fig:guidesample .}
elements.sent-2411-237: The last four columns of Fig.\ref{fig:guidesample illustrate the cluster formulation, which effectively separates clusters with watermarks from those without.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Additionally, our regularization design enables guided sampling to be performed exclusively within the no-watermark cluster.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/guidesample.png} \caption{We use Image Space L2 Loss to align the latent with the visual prompt while preserving the image's style. Oscillation separates clean and watermarked domains, and optimization yields a watermark-free result, demonstrating the effectiveness of Gaussian Processing Regularization.} \label{fig:guidesample}
elements.sent-2411-239: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:morecase , oscillation triggers a high-quality distribution in the 'Output' row from two or more 'weak' distributions, shown as the 'Input' and 'Augmented' rows.}
elements.sent-2411-240: The augmented distribution can be obtained from an off-the-shelf lightweight enhancer or image processing technique.
elements.sent-2411-241: The output is of high quality with extremely realistic style and texture, overcoming the over-smooth problem in GenAI images.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/image_enhancer.jpg} \caption{The inputs for the first three cases are low-resolution images enhanced using the Piscart API as augmented inputs. In the last two cases, the initial inputs are 64×64 low-scale images enhanced by the Piscart API, and the augmented inputs are the same low-scale images enhanced by the Magnific API. The results show that our method performs better in both real texture and style synthesis, as well as in identity preservation.} \label{fig:morecase}
elements.subsubsection-2411-007: Stroke Prompt Guided Recolor Qualitive Results and Make-up transfer Quantitive Results
elements.sent-2411-242: Our proposed method, oscillation group inversion with fine-tuning, is designed to support a variety of general low-level editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-243: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:makeup , rough strokes from doodle drawings serve as guides for fine-tuning, while text prompts direct semantic details.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Although our method can achieve effects like relighting and recolorization, these tasks are more challenging to evaluate due to the intuitive and non-precise nature of the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-245: Therefore, we primarily focus on makeup synthesis and transfer for validation.
elements.sent-2411-246: In the first part of our experiments, we use stroke prompts to create new makeup styles by altering facial and hair features.
elements.sent-2411-247: We compare our results with two recent makeup transfer methods, CSDMT (\cite{csdmt ) and Stable-Makeup (\cite{stable-makeup ).}}
elements.sent-2411-248: In the second part, we take the 'before' and 'after' makeup images generated from the style transfer results as low-level prompts, using them to produce higher-quality makeup images.
elements.sent-2411-249: This demonstrates our method’s ability to capture complex color distributions.
elements.sent-2411-250: Visual results are shown in Figure \ref{fig:makeup and Figure \ref{fig:makeupbig , and quantitative results are presented in Table~\ref{makeuptable .}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: These results follow the metrics used by CSDMT for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-252: The stroke editing experiments were conducted on a manually labeled dataset, which includes 10 high-quality face images and 77 different stroke variations, encompassing both makeup and hair color changes.
elements.sent-2411-253: The enhancement experiments were performed on the LADN dataset.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/makeup.jpg} \caption{Oscillation triggers a high-quality output of facial makeup harmonization through stroke prompts.} \label{fig:makeupbig}
elements.sent-2411-254: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:relight , our method can also create a harmonized lighting effect by applying group inversion from two images: one is the initial dark one, and the other is a visual prompt with doodle lighting, which can be hand-crafted or created by an AI agent.}
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/relight.pdf} \caption{The first three columns of cases show that the relighting can be harmonized and delicately controlled for different areas. The last three columns of cases show that the relighting can be applied for different colors and even creative effects like the last case, 'Christmas'.} \label{fig:relight}
elements.figure-2411-017: \begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|} Methods PSNR Stroke SSIM Stroke PSNR Enhance SSIM Enhance SDEdit(~\cite{meng2021sdedit}) 19.32 0.719 23.45 0.842 SDXL-turbo i2i 20.05 0.733 24.10 0.851 Flux 18.89 0.710 22.90 0.830 Oinv Ours 20.45 0.785 25.10 0.89 CSDMT(~\cite{csdmt}) 19.90 0.715 25.32 0.86 Stable-Makeup(~\cite{stable-makeup}) 18.60 0.705 24.30 0.884 \end{tabular} \caption{Comparison of different methods on the task of stroke make-up transfer and enhancement. The metrics demonstrate that our method is robust in stroke makeup transfer and significantly improves the quality of the baseline method compared to others.} \label{makeuptable}
